segments.s123-g73: همه پاک is an emphatic phrase meaning 'all together' or 'entirely' (پاک here is adverbial). با ... و با ... lists two means: 'with gifts and with offerings' (با + noun = 'with'). نثار is used as an odonym/noun 'offering/tribute'. The whole segment sets up the manner in which everyone came.
segments.s123-g74: کشیدند is a past tense plural verb 'they drew/formed' here in the sense 'draw a line / arrange in ranks' (an action performed by the subject). صف is the direct object 'a row/line'. بر در takes بر in the sense 'toward/to' and در is 'gate/door', so بر در شهریار means 'to/at the king's gate' (شهریار = 'king'). Note the verb precedes its object phrase for poetic word order but the sense is straightforward: 'they drew up a line at the king's gate.'
segments.s123-g75: This clause has subject + simile + copula + predicate: جهان 'the world' + چون بهشتی 'as/like a paradise' uses چون = 'like'. شد is the past copula 'became', and آراسته is a predicate adjective 'adorned/arrayed', describing the resulting state.
segments.s123-g76: Two parallel adjectival phrases describe the world's state: پر از داد = 'full of justice' (پر 'full (of)' + از 'of' + داد 'justice'), and آگنده از خواسته = 'filled with desire/requests' (آگنده 'filled/laden' + از 'with/of' + خواسته 'desire'). The و simply links the two descriptions.
segments.s123-g77: سر آمد here functions as a verbal idiom 'came to an end' (سر آمدن = 'to finish/end'). کنون 'now' gives the time, and رزم مازندران is the subject/oblique 'the battle/war of Mazandaran'. Word order is slightly inverted for emphasis but the sense is 'Now the Mazandaran war has ended.'
segments.s123-g78: به پیش here marks direction/placement 'before/in the presence of' (به + پیش). آورم is first-person present/subjunctive used with an intentive/future sense 'I will bring' or 'I will cause to be brought.' جنگ هاماوران is the object 'the battle/war of Hamavaran' (poetic order places the verb before its object for emphasis).
segments.s123-g1: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when' introducing a clause. The clause follows SOV order: proper name (کاووس) + locative phrase (در شهر ایران) + verb (رسید) in the simple past 'arrived'.
segments.s123-g2: ز here is the poetic 'from / because of'. گرد سپه functions as a noun phrase 'the dust (گرد) of the host/army (سپه)'. The predicate is structured as noun (هوا) + adjective (ناپدید) + auxiliary/verb (شد), i.e. 'the air became vanished.'
segments.s123-g3: برآمد is a past/epic perfect 'arose/issued forth'. همی is a classical aspectual/emphatic particle (here marking the vivid/ongoing nature of the action). تا ... signals extent 'as far as / even to' (تا به خورشید), with خورشید 'sun' as the target and جوش 'surge/boiling' describing the intensity.
segments.s123-g4: This clause lists the people (زن و مرد 'women and men') and uses شد ('became/there were') as the past copula: 'women and men came/stood before him.' پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of him', and با خروش is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with a cry/uproar.'
segments.s123-g5: همه شهر ایران is a noun phrase 'the whole city of Iran' (همه modifying شهر ایران). بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arrayed (themselves or it)', typical epic past tense with plural subject.
segments.s123-g6: This is a coordinated direct-object list (می‌و رود و رامشگران) governed by the past verb خواستند 'they wanted/asked for'. Here رود is used as a musical instrument ('string instrument'), رامشگران are 'entertainers/minstrels', and می‌و is listed as a festive provision (literal 'fruit' in the given senses, used as an item they requested).
segments.s123-g7: جهان سر به سر نو شد is idiomatic: سر به سر = 'entirely / all over', so the phrase means 'the world became wholly new.' از شاه نو is a prepositional phrase indicating source or cause 'because of / from the new king.' The verb شد is the past 'became.'
segments.s123-g8: ز ایران برآمد is 'arose from Iran' (ز = from). یکی ماه نو is a nominal phrase 'one/new moon' (یکی here 'one/a certain'), with ماه 'moon' used poetically (often for a splendid/new ruler) and نو 'new' as adjective; برآمد is the past 'arose/appeared.'
segments.s123-g9: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when” introducing a clause. بر تخت is a prepositional phrase (بر = on/upon + تخت = throne) and بنشست is the third-person singular past (epic/archaic narrative) ‘sat down’; پیروز و شاد are post-verbal adjectives describing the (omitted) subject (the king). The subject pronoun is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s123-g10: در marks location/goal (“in/into”) and introduces the noun phrase گنجهای کهن (plural ‘treasury‑vaults / ancient treasuries’). برگشاد is an archaic/past verbal form (‘opened’) placed after the noun phrase, with its (same) subject understood from the previous clause.
segments.s123-g11: ز هر جای is a prepositional phrase ‘from every place’; روزی‌دهان را is the definite/direct object (را marks it) meaning ‘the providers/givers of daily sustenance’; بخواند is the third-person singular narrative/past form here meaning ‘he summoned/called’. The verb follows the object as typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s123-g12: به introduces the beneficiary/goal (‘into/to the…’). دیوان here means the official register/office; دینار is the coin being distributed; دادن appears as a verbal noun/infinitive ‘giving’; نشاند is an archaic past form meaning ‘he caused/arranged (them to give / to be seated to give)’. Together the sequence expresses that he arranged for dinars to be given into the diwan/record.
segments.s123-g13: برآمد is a past intransitive verb ‘arose/issued forth’ and takes the noun خروش (‘roar/uproar’) as its semantic subject. از در پیلتن is a locative/source phrase ‘from the gate of Pilten’ (در here = ‘gate’). Word order places the verb first for vivid narration.
segments.s123-g14: بزرگان لشکر is a genitive construction ‘the great ones/elders of the army’ (بزرگان modifies لشکر). شدند is the third-person plural past of شدن (‘became / came to be’) and انجمن is the nominal complement ‘an assembly’ — together ‘they formed/constituted an assembly’ (i.e. they gathered).
segments.s123-g15: همه شادمان is the subject phrase ‘all the rejoicing/happy ones’. نزد شاه آمدند is a prepositional phrase ‘came to/into the presence of the king’ (نزد = in the presence of) followed by آمدند, the third-person plural past ‘they came’.
segments.s123-g16: بران is an adverbial/deictic referring back to that place/occasion (literally ‘to/onto that’); نامور modifies the implied people as ‘renowned’; پیشگاه is ‘the (royal) presence/court’. آمدند is again the past plural verb ‘they came’ — together: ‘to that renowned presence the famed ones came.’
segments.s123-g25: صد + از + ماهرویان is a partitive nominal phrase: 'a hundred of the moon‑faced (ones)'. The word order is numeral + از (partitive 'of') + plural noun; زرین کمر is a post‑nominal adjectival phrase ('golden‑waisted' / 'with golden girdles') that qualifies ماهرویان. There is no finite verb in this line — it is a descriptive noun phrase in a catalogue of possessions/people.
segments.s123-g26: Again a partitive construction: صد از مشک مویان = 'a hundred of the musk‑haired (ones)'. مشک modifies مویان (hair), i.e. 'whose hair is like/has musk'. با introduces the accompanying qualities زیب و فر ('with beauty and farr'); note the coordinate structure زیب و فر follows با and describes the state/ornament of these people.
segments.s123-g27: صد از اسپ = 'a hundred of the horses' (partitive); با links the following items as accompaniments: زین و زرین ستام = 'saddles and golden ___ (trappings)'. The adjective زرین modifies ستام. The exact lexical role of ستام is archaic/uncertain here, so the segment functions as a noun phrase enumerating equipment accompanying the horses.
segments.s123-g28: صد استر ... is another nominal group parallel to the previous one: 'a hundred mares'. سیه موی is a compound adjective 'black‑haired' (سیه modifying موی), and و زرین لگام coordinates another attribute 'and with golden bridles' where زرین modifies لگام. The nouns follow their modifiers (typical Persian poetic order).
segments.s123-g29: همه بارشان = 'all their load' (همه quantifies بار with the possessive suffix -شان 'their'). دیبه خسروی is a noun phrase 'royal brocade/royal silk' that serves as the material/content of that load; the segment is a nominal apposition/predication without an explicit verb (catalog style).
segments.s123-g30: This is a short origin list introduced by the poetic preposition ز/از: 'from China and Rûm and from Pahlavi.' The conjunction و coordinates the origins; the repeated prepositions (ز / از) mark source/origin of the brocades.
segments.s123-g31: ببردند is a 3rd‑person plural past verb 'they took / they carried.' The object is صد بدره دینار — numeral + an attributive بدره (a poetic epithet meaning 'splendid/like the full moon') modifying دینار. نیز ('also') adds this item to the catalogue. The clause is a simple past action listing what they bore.
segments.s123-g32: A distributive list introduced repeatedly by ز (poetic 'of/from'): 'of colour and of scent and of every kind of thing.' The repeated ز before each noun is a rhetorical device enumerating different kinds; هرگونه modifies چیز to mean 'every kind of thing.'
segments.s123-g57: This is a sequence of prepositional/time phrases followed by the verb: 'به طوس' = 'to/at Tus' (preposition + place), then the temporal 'آن زمان' = 'at that time', and finally 'داد اسپهبدی' where 'داد' is the past verb 'gave' and 'اسپهبدی' is the nominal title 'the spahbed-ship' (an office). Word order is topical/place → time → predicate, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s123-g58: 'بدو گفت' literally 'he said to him' (بدو = to him as indirect object). The verb 'گفت' here functions as the reporting verb ('said'); the provided lexical senses did not include the verbal sense, so that entry is marked -1. The clause that follows, 'از ایران بگردان بدی', is an imperative: 'بگردان' = 'turn away / drive out' (imperative), with 'از ایران' marking the source/territory ('from Iran'), and 'بدی' = 'evil' as the object being turned away.
segments.s123-g59: 'پس آنگه' are consecutive temporal adverbs (both mean 'then/after that'), emphasizing sequence. 'سپاهان' here is the plural noun 'armies' (the thing given) and 'به گودرز داد' = 'gave to Goudarz' with 'به گودرز' marking the indirect object and 'داد' the past tense verb 'gave'. The direct object (سپاهان) precedes the indirect object, a common Persian order.
segments.s123-g60: 'ورا' = 'to him' (indirect object). The nouns 'کام و فرمان' are coordinated objects: 'کام' (desire/privilege/satisfaction) and 'فرمان' (command/order). 'آن مرز' ('that frontier') specifies what the command concerns. The final 'داد' is again the verb 'gave/granted' — together: 'he granted him the privileges and the command of that frontier.'
segments.s123-g61: 'وزان پس' = 'and from that (time) on' — a temporal connector. 'به شادی و می‌دست برد' is a poetic construction: 'به شادی' = 'with joy', 'می‌دست' is an archaic imperfective/characterizing form with 'می-' indicating habitual/continuous action and 'دست' = 'hand', and 'برد' here functions as a verbal root meaning 'took/seized' (used as a past/habitual sense). Together it describes habitual/characteristic action: 'with joy and by hand he seized.'
segments.s123-g62: 'جهان را' marks 'the world' as the definite object (را). 'نموده' is a perfect/participial form 'having made/done' (here functioning like a perfect participle meaning 'he effected/committed'), followed by 'بسی' (an adverb meaning 'much/many' in classical Persian; the provided senses did not match that meaning so it is marked -1) and 'دستبرد' = 'seizure/plunder'. The phrase means 'he made many seizures of the world' (i.e., he plundered or took much from the world).
segments.s123-g63: 'بزد' is the past tense 'he struck/smote'. The direct object is 'گردن غم' = 'the neck of sorrow' (a metaphorical object). 'به شمشیر' uses 'به' to mark instrument ('with the sword') and the closing 'داد' is the verbal 'gave/caused' often used in classical Persian in combination with another verb to complete the action (here emphasizing the force/completion of the blow). Overall it reads idiomatically as 'he struck sorrow's neck with the sword.'
segments.s123-g64: 'نیامد' = 'did not come' (negated past), with the particle 'همی' adding a classical/poetic emphasis ('ever/still'). 'بر دل' = 'upon the heart' (locative/experiential), and 'از مرگ یاد' = 'remembrance of death' (از marks the source/contents of the remembrance). The whole clause: 'the remembrance of death did not come upon his heart.'
segments.s123-g49: خروش (noun) is the subject meaning 'uproar/tumult'; تبیره follows and qualifies that noun (a poetic adjective/epithet attached after the noun). برآمد is the past tense (third-person singular) 'issued forth/rose'. ز is the poetic form of از marking source ('from'), and شهر is the source 'the city'. Word order (Noun + modifier + verb + prepositional source) is typical of Ferdowsi's classical style.
segments.s123-g50: ز شادی is a prepositional/causal phrase 'from/of joy' (giving the content or cause). به هرکس marks the indirect object 'to each/every person' (به + هرکس). رسانید is the past tense verb 'conveyed/delivered' (third-person singular). بهر at the end is an archaic/poetic postposition meaning 'for' or 'for the sake of' and ties the clause to purpose or beneficiary.
segments.s123-g51: بشد is an archaic/poetic past/preterite-like verb meaning 'it happened / they came to be' (here introducing appearance/arrival). رستم زال can be read as the names 'Rostam and Zal' placed together; the coordination is implied by context. و joins the next clause; بنشست is the past 'sat down' (verb before the subject شاه, a common poetic inversion), so 'the king sat (down)'.
segments.s123-g52: جهان is the grammatical subject 'the world'; کرد is the simple past 'made/caused'; روشن is the predicate adjective 'bright/clear' (so 'made the world bright'). به is the instrumental/causal preposition 'by/with' here, and آیین و راه are the instruments/means 'custom/rite and way/path' describing how the world was made bright.
segments.s123-g53: به شادی is a circumstantial prepositional phrase 'in/with joy'. بر تخت زرین is the locative expression 'upon the golden throne' (بر + تخت + adjective زرین). نشست is the past verb 'sat down' (third-person singular); the subject is understood from prior context.
segments.s123-g54: همی is a poetic particle here that emphasizes or accompanies the action (often found in classical Persian to mark continuity or immediacy). جور و بیداد را is the definite object 'injustice and oppression' (را marks the object). در ببست is a compound verbal phrase 'closed/shut the door' (در as 'door' + ببست 'he shut'), used metaphorically to mean he put an end to injustice.
segments.s123-g55: زمین را is the definite direct object 'the land' (را marks definiteness). ببخشید is the past verb 'bestowed/gave' (here transitive 'gave as a grant'). بر introduces the beneficiary 'to/upon', and مهتران (plural) means 'the great ones/chiefs' — the recipients of the grant.
segments.s123-g56: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. باز آمد is a compound past meaning 'came back/returned' (باز 'again' + آمد 'came'). از شهر مازندران is a simple source phrase 'from the city of Mazandaran' (از marks origin; مازندران is the place-name).
segments.s123-g33: The line opens with the poetic preposition ز (’from/of’) marking material or source: ز یاقوت جامی = 'a goblet of ruby' (i.e. a ruby goblet). جامی (a cup/goblet) is indefinite here, and پر از + N is a standard Persian pattern meaning 'full of N' (پر = 'full', followed by the preposition از + noun). ناب is an adjective after the noun (مشک ناب = 'pure musk'), a common word order in classical Persian.
segments.s123-g34: Again introduced by the poetic ز, ز پیروزه دیگر یکی = 'from another turquoise (one)', where پیروزه (turquoise) and دیگر ('other/another') form 'the other turquoise (cup)'. یکی functions as an indefinite/pronominal 'one' (= 'a certain/another one'). پر گلاب uses the same 'full of' construction as before: 'full of rosewater'.
segments.s123-g35: نوشته here functions as a past verbal form ('wrote' / 'written') used in a narratorial/poetic sense. یکی نامه‌ای = 'a certain/a single letter' (یکی + نامه‌ای makes it indefinite/emphatic). بر حریر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on/over silk' that modifies where the letter was written (or the material of the letter).
segments.s123-g36: This is an enumerative phrase of perfumes introduced repeatedly by the poetic preposition ز (’with/of/from’): ز مشک و ز عنبر ز عود و عبیر = 'with musk and with ambergris, with oud and (other) fragrance'. The repetition of ز is a stylistic (poetic) device to list ingredients/scents; عبیر here is the general word 'fragrance'.
segments.s123-g37: سپرد is the simple past transitive verb 'entrusted/handed over' with این as its direct object. The recipient is marked by به + title: به سالار گیتی فروز = 'to the commander (سالار) Giti‑Frouz' (گیتی فروز is a compound epithet/title attached to سالار). Word order is verb — object — indirect object (typical in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s123-g38: This phrase specifies the office/assignment: به نوی همه کشور نیمروز. به = 'to/for', نوی = 'new' (here implying 'as the new [ruler/governor]'), followed by همه کشور نیمروز = 'the whole country/province of Nimruz' (همه quantifies کشور and نیمروز is the region name). The noun denoting the office (e.g. حاکم) is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s123-g39: چنان کز is a fixed classical/poetic conjunction meaning 'so that/so' (چنان that). پس here means 'after' and عهد is 'era/reign' (age). کاووس شاه = 'King Kavus' (name + title). The whole clause introduces a result/time clause: 'so that after the reign of King Kavus...'.
segments.s123-g40: نباشد is the negative present/jussive form 'there shall not be / will not be' (classical/poetic usage). بران تخت = 'on that throne' (بران is an archaic demonstrative/adverb meaning 'on/thereon'), and کس را کلاه literally 'anyone's hat' — the order is figurative: 'no one's hat will be on that throne', i.e. 'no one will sit on/occupy that throne.' را is the object/topic marker attached after کس.
segments.s123-g65: زمین گشت is a topicalized order (literally 'the earth became'), where گشت is the past of شدن 'become'. پر here is the adjectival particle 'full (of)' used poetically without the expected از, followed by a coordinated list سبزه و آب و نم 'grass, water, and dew' that specifies what the earth is full of.
segments.s123-g66: بیاراست is a past/ perfective verb meaning 'adorned' (3sg); it takes گیتی 'the world' as its logical subject/object here in an inverted order. چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and باغ ارم names the famous paradise (ارم) to which the world is compared.
segments.s123-g67: توانگر شد is a compound predicate 'became wealthy' (شد = became). The phrases introduced by از ('from/through') — از داد و از ایمنی — indicate cause or means ('through justice and through safety'), with the repeated از used for emphasis.
segments.s123-g68: ز بد is an archaic/poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from/because of evil'. بسته شد دست اهریمنی is a passive/resultative pattern: بسته (bound/tied) + شد (became/was) with دست اهریمنی 'the demonic hand' as the entity affected — i.e. 'the demon's hand/power was bound'.
segments.s123-g69: به گیتی خبر شد is an impersonal/experiential construction 'it became news to the world' or 'word reached the world'; شد is the past of شدن used here as 'it was reported/it became known'. که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that Kaavus the king ...'.
segments.s123-g70: ز مازندران gives the source/location 'from Mazandaran'. بستد is past (3sg) of بستن here in a transitive/figurative sense 'seized/secured/took', and آن تاج و گاه is the direct object 'that crown and (throne/seat)'; گاه is archaic/poetic for 'seat' or 'throne' (not the basic 'time' sense), so its listed senses do not match exactly.
segments.s123-g71: بماندند is past plural 'they remained' or 'they were left' and here serves to describe the people's reaction. یکسر همه 'all entirely' is an adverbial/emphatic fronting, and زین شگفت ('at/from this wonder') contains زین as an archaic poetic form meaning 'from this/at this' (not 'saddle'), so the given dictionary senses don't capture its use here.
segments.s123-g72: که introduces the clause giving the cause of astonishment. کاووس شاه is the subject 'King Kavus'; این بزرگی 'this greatness' is the thing attained, and گرفت (past of گرفتن) means 'seized/attained' — here 'he achieved this greatness'. Word order places subject before object and verb at the end, a normal arrangement in poetic Persian.
segments.s123-g41: مگر here functions as an adversative 'but/rather' introducing a contrast. نامور رستم زال را is a definite object phrase: نامور (adjective) precedes the proper name رستم, with زال in apposition (lineage) and the enclitic را marking the entire phrase as definite/object. The order (adjective—name—apposition—را) is a common poetic ordering.
segments.s123-g42: خداوند is the agent/subject 'the Lord/God' doing the giving; the verb 'gave' is implied (a frequent poetic ellipsis) so the clause reads '[but] the Lord gave...'. شمشیر و گوپال را is the coordinated definite object (شمشیر and گوپال) marked by را; و simply coordinates the two nouns. Note the poetic inversion and omission of an explicit verb.
segments.s123-g43: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that/thereupon'. برو is the imperative 'go'. آفرین کرد is a common classical/perhaps archaic phrase meaning 'uttered acclamation' or 'cried “bravo”'; کرد is the past tense auxiliary 'did/made' attached to that phrase. شاه is the subject performing the action. Word order (adverbial—imperative—acclaim—کرد—subject) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s123-g44: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the king's reason or assertion. بی‌تو 'without you' sets the conditional/context, and مبیناد is a present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'would be seen/appear' (here rendered as 'would appear' or 'be seen'); کس, because of the negative/limiting context, functions as 'no one' (sense conveyed by the whole clause), and پیشگاه means 'in the presence (of the king)/before the court'. Together: 'that without you no one will appear before the court.'
segments.s123-g45: دل تاجداران is a genitive/possessive notion 'the hearts of the crown-bearers' (in verse the ezāfe is omitted). به تو is the direction 'toward you'; گرم is the predicative adjective 'warm/fervent' and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (a wish/blessing). The construction expresses a benediction: 'May the rulers' hearts be warm to you.'
segments.s123-g46: روانت is روان (soul/spirit) with the 2nd-person possessive suffix -ت 'your soul'. پر از is the idiom 'full of'; شرم و آزرم are coordinated abstract nouns 'shame and modesty/reverence' (آزرم is an older/less-common word here meaning modesty/reverence). باد again is the optative 'may'. The clause is another blessing: 'May your soul be full of shame and modesty.'
segments.s123-g47: فرو برد is a compound verbal phrase (فرو + برد) meaning 'bent down/stooped' or 'lowered (himself)'; here it describes the first action. رستم is the subject performing the actions. ببوسید تخت is a second finite verb 'he kissed the throne' (ببوسید past tense) with تخت as its object. The sequence gives two successive past actions by Rostam; the placement of رستم between the verbs is a poetic word-order variant.
segments.s123-g48: بسیچ is an intensifier 'many/very' (here 'much/many a'), گذر کرد is a periphrastic past 'made a passage/passed through' (گذر = passage, کرد = did/made). و connects the clauses. بربست is a past verb 'bound/packed up' (بر + بست), and رخت is 'baggage/garments/belongings'. Together: 'he passed through many (places/people) and packed up his gear.'
segments.s123-g17: تهمتن is an epithet/proper name (used as the subject). بیامد is the simple past ‘came.’ The phrase به سر بر کلاه is a locative/descriptive phrase ‘(a) hat on (his) head’ — Persian often omits the possessive pronoun, and the sequence places the locative (به سر) before the noun (کلاه); this whole phrase links to the following verb (he arrived with a hat on his head).
segments.s123-g18: نشست is the past of ‘sit’/‘sit down.’ The string از بر تخت نزدیک شاه is a classical locative expression: از + بر + تخت indicates position in relation to the throne (literally ‘from/on the throne’ but idiomatically ‘by/beside the throne’) and نزدیک شاه specifies proximity to the king. Together they describe where he sat.
segments.s123-g19: سزاوار is an adjectival/predicative word ‘worthy/deserving.’ It precedes the pronoun او (he), so the phrase reads roughly ‘worthy was he’; شهریار زمین is an appositive noun phrase ‘the sovereign/king of the land’ that identifies the referent. Classical Persian often places the predicate before the pronoun for emphasis.
segments.s123-g20: یکی is the indefinite/one (here introducing a single item among gifts). خلعت is the direct object ‘robe of honour.’ آراست is the past verb ‘adorned/vested (him).’ با آفرین is an adverbial phrase ‘with praise/cheers’ (با = with, آفرین = acclamation), so the whole clause means ‘one robe of honour was bestowed/adorned upon him with acclamation.’
segments.s123-g21: This is an elliptical noun phrase introduced by یکی (‘one’). تخت is the head noun ‘throne,’ modified postpositively by پیروزه (‘turquoise’/turquoise‑inlaid). The conjunction و links the second item میش‌سار (an obscure/compound term listed as the second object). The verb/copula is omitted, typical in lists of gifts.
segments.s123-g22: Again elliptical: یکی introduces another item. خسروی is an adjectival/epithet ‘royal’ modifying تاج ‘crown.’ گوهر نگار functions as a compound describing the crown (literally ‘gem’ + نگار); in context it means the crown is adorned with gems. The copula is omitted as before.
segments.s123-g23: یکی again introduces an item. دست here is a noun used for a piece of clothing (classically ‘sleeve’ or ‘article’), modified by زربفت ‘gold‑brocaded’ and شاهنشهی ‘imperial’ (adjectival). The construction is elliptical (copula omitted): ‘one (was) an imperial gold‑brocaded garment.’
segments.s123-g24: This segment lists particular garments/accessories. ابا in the manuscript context is the clothing noun ‘aba’ (cloak) though the provided lemma senses did not include that reading (so marked below as not matching). یاره is another item in the list (the exact technical meaning is dialectal/obscure here). طوق is ‘collar/necklace.’ با = ‘with,’ and فرهی is an adjective/noun meaning ‘distinction, nobility’ (here describing the splendour of the items). The whole string is a coordinated list: ‘an aba, a (yārah) and a collar, with distinction.’

sentences.s123-s37: All together, cleanly bearing gifts and offerings, they drew up a line at the king's gate.
sentences.s123-s38: The world became like a paradise, adorned — full of justice and brimming with desire.
sentences.s123-s39: Now the Mazandaran campaign has come to an end; I will bring the battle of Hamavaran before you.
sentences.s123-s1: When Kavus arrived in the city of Iran, the air vanished in the dust raised by the host.
sentences.s123-s2: A tumult rose even up to the sun; women and men stood before him with a cry.
sentences.s123-s3: All the city of Iran was arrayed; they sought fruit, a stringed instrument, and entertainers.
sentences.s123-s4: The whole world became entirely new because of the new king; from Iran there arose a new moon.
sentences.s123-s5: When the victorious and joyful one sat upon the throne, he opened the ancient treasuries.
sentences.s123-s6: From every place he summoned providers, and had dinars delivered into the court register.
sentences.s123-s7: A roar arose from the gate of Pilten; the great men of the army formed an assembly.
sentences.s123-s8: All the rejoicing ones came before the king; the renowned (men) came into that presence.
sentences.s123-s13: A hundred of the moon‑faced with golden belts; a hundred of the musk‑haired, with beauty and royal glory.
sentences.s123-s14: A hundred horses with saddles and golden trappings; a hundred mares black‑haired and with golden bridles.
sentences.s123-s15: All their load was royal brocade, from China and Rûm and from Pahlavi.
sentences.s123-s16: They also carried a hundred splendid dinars, of colour and of scent and of every kind of thing.
sentences.s123-s29: At Tus, at that time he gave the spahbed-ship; he said to him, "Drive evil from Iran."
sentences.s123-s30: Then he entrusted the armies to Goudarz and granted him the satisfaction (privileges) and command of that frontier.
sentences.s123-s31: From that time on, rejoicing and seizing with his hand, he laid many hands on the world (made many seizures of the world).
sentences.s123-s32: He smote the neck of sorrow with his sword; the remembrance of death did not come upon his heart.
sentences.s123-s25: An uproar rose from the city, and it carried joy to every person.
sentences.s123-s26: Rostam and Zal appeared, and the king sat down; he made the world bright by custom and the right way.
sentences.s123-s27: In joy he sat upon the golden throne, and he shut the door on injustice and oppression.
sentences.s123-s28: He granted the land to the chiefs when he returned from the city of Mazandaran.
sentences.s123-s17: A ruby goblet filled with pure musk, and another turquoise one filled with rosewater.
sentences.s123-s18: He wrote a certain letter on silk, perfumed with musk and ambergris, with oud and other fragrance.
sentences.s123-s19: He entrusted this to the commander Giti-Frouz, as the new governor of the entire province of Nimruz.
sentences.s123-s20: So that after the era of King Kavus no one's hat would be on that throne.
sentences.s123-s33: The earth became full of grass, water, and dew; the world was adorned like the Garden of Iram.
sentences.s123-s34: People became wealthy through justice and security, and the demonic hand was bound from evil.
sentences.s123-s35: Word reached the world that King Kavus had taken that crown and throne from Mazandaran.
sentences.s123-s36: All were utterly astonished at this marvel that King Kavus had achieved such greatness.
sentences.s123-s21: But the Lord gave the famed Rostam, son of Zal, the sword and Gupāl.
sentences.s123-s22: After that the king cried 'Go, bravo!' saying that, without you, no one would appear before the court.
sentences.s123-s23: May the hearts of the crown-bearers be warm toward you; may your soul be full of shame and modesty.
sentences.s123-s24: Rostam bent low, kissed the throne, passed on through many (crowds/places), and packed up his belongings.
sentences.s123-s9: Tahmatan came, put a hat upon his head, and sat down beside the king's throne.
sentences.s123-s10: The sovereign of the land was worthy, and he was adorned with a robe of honour amid acclamation.
sentences.s123-s11: One was a turquoise throne and a mish-sar; another a royal crown studded with gems.
sentences.s123-s12: Another was an imperial gold‑brocaded garment: an aba, a yārah and a collar, all splendid.
